speaker: interviewer
text: It's tape number three, and we are interviewing Mr. Yehudah Bakon. Now, we stopped at your bar mitzvah. We went back to Ostrava.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you wanted to tell us about your bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I prepared the bar mitzvah during, more or less, after the Heydrich affair in '42. And my haftarah was Jeremiah 1. And I had to learn it by heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was forbidden to have any minyan, any, any gathering. So what to do? We had a friend who lived near us. And he had an old small house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in their washroom, we had secretly the bar mitzvah, the Shacharit. And that was very dangerous. And I remember just when I started my bar mitzvah, in the courtyard, the man who collects rubbish came in to collect it and peeped through a tiny little window into the washroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were terribly afraid, because he saw strange people with a tallit, with a shawl, a praying shawl. What will be? So quickly we finished our prayers, and secretly we disappeared. Yeah? Dispersed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dispersed. And of course, at that time, it was so dangerous that I remember even if we were caught on the street, even if we were only two or three, we had to know about we-- what we were talking about. Because the Germans separated us and asked, what were you talking about? And it had to be something--

speaker: interviewer
text: The same thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. So that memory is very vivid in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember something from your haftarah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Jeremiah?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING HEBREW] I am with you to save you, says God. And this theme, together with another theme, a few Latin words I learned in Theresienstadt-- [SPEAKING LATIN]. As long as I breathe, I believe-- kept me somehow also alive. Because I always came back to this, and later on, I made many drawings of it. Now we are going--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are going back to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: --back to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came and your first impressions were really very horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very horrible. And-- it-- nearly despair and so on. But after a while--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were not alone. You were with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were. With my--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were together.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was very, very difficult-- the situation. And the very first time in my life without-- a few hours, even-- without my parents and to look for my sister. But later on came my uncle, who was living in Magdeburg-Kaserne. He was the chief cantor in this Magdeburg. And he knew very well all high officials. He knew very well Kurt Loewenstein, who was the head of the ghetto [NON-ENGLISH], whom I later also knew very well. He knew very well Edelstein, the head of the Jews. He was living not far away with-- from the dwellings of Edelstein and [NON-ENGLISH], and Janovitz, who was the head of the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came and helped us. How he helped us? He, himself, was living in a huge stall with many other people. But he had a kind of a few meters where he could squeeze in my father and me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother and sister were still, for a few days, in this horrible place, in the Magdeburg-Kaserne. Later, they came to the Dredsner-Kaserne, to the Dredsner barracks for women. My father came later to the Hamburger-Kaserne barracks. And I, because I knew very well the son of Jacob Edelstein, Aryeh, who was my friend still from '39, we had to use kind of-- I would call it, for holiday--

speaker: interviewer
text: Camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: From youth camp. And then I met Edelstein and his son. And since then, we were the best friends. And I was many times, for a long period, at his house. I mean, house. He had one and a half room, which was one of the greatest luxury for Theresienstadt. But I could play and stay with him. All other people had only tiny little-- the highest--

speaker: interviewer
text: Luxury.

speaker: interviewee
text: --luxury was to have a half room of privacy. The normal prisoner or people lived in horrible conditions. And even we children-- I mean, I came because, as I told you, I knew Edelstein. And he knew very well, and Fredy Hirsch, who was the head of the youth movement--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Theresienstadt. I became to know him. And he arranged a place for me in the youth or the children house, L417. Not all children were lucky and came into these children home. These children home were designed for children about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Only boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12 and about 15 or so. Older children from 16 till 18 were in [GERMAN]. We were in Kinderheim, in children home. And the other one, in [GERMAN], they had to work a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's only boys, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were also--

speaker: interviewer
text: Girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Adequate homes. L410, I think, was for the girls. And it was the same for German speaking. So there were some buildings which were for this kind of children. And of course, they were also for very small children, a babies home. But I can tell you more about the children home. Because I was in one of these children home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the babies were not with their parents? With their mothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The babies were with their parents, but there were many orphaned also. They were also-- they cared for the orphan. But most of the children who were in Theresienstadt didn't have this luxury we had. They were living with the old people somewhere on the roof, under very, very bad condition. Now, coming to this children home gave us much better condition. Because that we knew later on, Edelstein's philosophy was that the youth are the hope of the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: The future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt had the possibility of so-called self-government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, of the autonomy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Autonomy. Of course, it was a big bluff. Because the German decided all of us should be, in the end killed. But meanwhile, it meant that if the German didn't say it in a different way, they could choose the Jews, the Judenrat, who is going to the east. They could choose these 5,000 people to the east. Sometimes the German gave directive. Let's say, when they said, 10,000 old people, it had to be old people from-- let's say, from 60. Or if they said, only this and this category of people. Or in this transport, they shouldn't be people who had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Red hair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Special order, [NON-ENGLISH]-- how we say it? First-- some cross-- from the First War. It was soldiers we had this kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Distinction.

speaker: interviewee
text: Distinction. Or in Theresienstadt there were some great scientists, [NON-ENGLISH], we called them. And they had special dwellings. And they had to write postcards. And they were even allowed to have some correspondence with other scientists abroad. Because they put pressure on the International Red Cross, and the German need it for their purpose. Maybe about this a little more later. Anyhow, we children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had this advantage of the policy of Edelstein that we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: More--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was symbolically. One piece or some-- a little more food. But the old people got a little less. Which meant for them, starvation. For us, it meant survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cruel decision. And I only say what we know. That's what Edelstein and maybe some other people decided. I remember that when we got our food in the beginning-- I mean, that is a child of 13-- we were in a queue, and the food was distributed. And you got the kind of tickets. And some number would cut out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, rations. And here you are. And you get a horrible soup, and some two potatoes, and a sauce. Only that was that. And the soup was so horrible that we children didn't want it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eat it.

speaker: interviewee
text: To eat it. And next to us were old people-- mainly the German people. Why? Because the Czech people, the Czech old people had it a tiny little better. They knew the language. And most of the people in Theresienstadt were Czech speaking. Though there were German, and even some Dutch, and later on Danish. But the people who knew only German were terribly hit by the situation. I mean, they were promised to come in a olderly-- a home for elderly people. They paid for reservations. They could choose a room with their son from this side and so on. I mean, tremendous bluff. And suddenly, they were used that a promise is a promise and a word is a word. And suddenly, they arrive in Theresienstadt and they came into the most horrible dwellings and under most horrible conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you gave them the soup?

speaker: interviewee
text: So these people are staying here. Shabby, in our eyes. Dirty and unpleasant. And I remember they were always saying, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Are you taking the soup? And we gave them our ration of our soup. At that time we were not so hungry. And we gave it to them. But for a child, without much philosophy or possibility of reflection, these were like beggars. Very-- sometimes stinky, sometime--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabby. But we had, of course, a compassion. And we gave them this soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate only the potatoes or whatever was. And we somehow had it a little better than them. Later on, the children and our youth leaders even, made a kind of [SPEAKING HEBREW], The. Helping hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it? We went to these old-people dwellings, and we took them out. We took their mattresses-- Mattresses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattresses. And we aired it a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: You cleaned it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we cleaned it a little. Sometimes it was full of louses and so on. And this kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Helping, sort of.

speaker: interviewee
text: The helping, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. We knew what is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was organized, you're saying.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized. But on the other hand, we couldn't as 13-year-old children actually really understand their suffering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me, did you see your family, your parents, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- the ghetto in the beginning, changed several times. First of all, there was a [NON-ENGLISH]. People couldn't go--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: --out and things. And in the beginning, even a part of the Czech Christian community was living nearby. Slowly, all of them were evacuated. And the ghetto was only a Jewish ghetto. And later, this [NON-ENGLISH] or this kind of curfew was opened. Sometimes the curfew was only in certain hours. But in my time, it was opened. And we could meet our parents after the working hours. My mother worked. And my father worked in a kind of [NON-ENGLISH]. They've done all kinds of things from paper for the Germans. And I could see them. And later on, I could even help them a little. Because I had a little better food. And I brought to my mother and to my father something of my ration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all the time, transports were leaving for Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The greatest fear was the transport to the east. We didn't know what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it meant that nobody comes back. We have nothing. Have no-- no correspondence of any sort at that time. And suddenly, you are in a children room, apropos. If you want, I will tell you more how it was organized, the life in such a children room. The life was rather well organized. Again, not for all children. Only for--

speaker: interviewer
text: For us-- for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For various. We were-- it was a former school. And the classes-- let's see. My class had about 36 children, and we had a very organized life. We were sleeping in three bunkers. And we had a mat--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had a bed for yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a bed. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a bench?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bench-- but a double bench. There were only a few single benches, but most of them were double benches. And three--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you slept with another boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sometimes I was lucky. In my case, I had this bench where there were only three. But we had no--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had-- you had normal-- you wore pajamas to go to sleep? How did you dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had what we had still with us. We could change some clothes here and there. We could go to a kind of washing place. But it was a shower for few minutes. And it was a whole ceremony, because we got a special kind of ticket. And all of us went.

speaker: interviewer
text: To take a shower?

speaker: interviewee
text: To shower. If you're lucky, the shower was even for a couple of minutes. And if you're even luckier--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was warm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warm, yeah. And so on. And even sometimes, they repaired our shoes. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you cover yourself at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had still from home some blankets. And you could organize some--

speaker: interviewer
text: And also pillows?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember pillows. But maybe there was something we put under. But all our belongings was on the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was on the bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had even illegal teaching. We had some wonderful teachers. But if you remember, since '42, teaching in protectorate-- every occupied place in Germany had a different kind of law-- was forbidden. And I remember always, one of our boys was outside. And if SS men was around, he quickly came in. And we didn't have exercise books, but we had some papers from parcels and so on. We put it under our mattress. And we knew exactly what is the subject we speak about, some neutral thing that, our madrich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say, about a river, or a story, and so on-- but neutral. But in fact--

speaker: interviewer
text: You are learning.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a kind of education. And we had the best people possible. And the reason is that everyone wanted to be a madrich or a teacher in this children home. Why? It was in a higher priority of [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] means a list, a protectory list against transfer to the east. Because when there was a transport, the Judenrat and others were divided. I mean, there was the Czech Jews, the German Jews, even a little Christian community. I mean, they didn't know that they are Jew. But they were in Theresienstadt with even Catholic praying and so on. And every group had somebody in the Judenrat, in this place where they decided who should be sent. And the children block had higher protege. They were on a-- somehow covered better against this transport to the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do I know it? Because as I was staying with Aryeh Edelstein, and once when this transport list was ready, Edelstein got a copy of the list. And I was able to look with Aryeh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing was I was looking if my family and I am in the list. But as long Edelstein was with us, he kept us clear from the transport to the east. But people with higher-- in higher functions or madrichim had better chance to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: To survive, to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They didn't got money, but they had better protections.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Edelstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Therefore, the very best--

speaker: interviewer
text: Teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Teachers were-- I remember one teacher was a former professor of history in Brunn. The other teacher was even the assistant of Einstein in physics from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of who?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Einstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Einstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: Noel or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: He taught you physics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, basic-- we were children. Or mathematics or whatever we thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we learned a few words in Latin. The first time I heard, I would say, French Revolution, Robespierre, and Danton, and I don't know, was in Theresienstadt. We had some education. I heard about Herzl and about Ahad Ha'am. We even had Erev Shabbat, a kind of Oneg Shabbat where madrichim told us stories in the room. There were all kinds of rooms. The things that Zionists--

speaker: interviewer
text: Organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Madrichim-- it was more underlined, the education for Palestine or about Harutzim and some--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start painting there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you about it. And in other rooms, there were Czech Jews and the Communists. And they published a wonderful newspaper, which is apropos today. Everything survived with them. That was room number one. I happened to be there for a short time. And there were wonderful, most talented boys who wrote poetry, and drew, and had the stories. And we had, like scouts, a competition. And you asked about my drawings. One of the competition was to do some handicrafts or some drawings. And if you were good, you got some points. And your room won to competition and so on. Or didn't-- or the other one won the competition. And so your life in this youth-- children block-- was like a scout thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was nice altogether. Not too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a way-- comparatively. And one of this competition was drawing. And I made drawing of the courtyards and some people. And that was the very first time somebody offered me a half a loaf of bread, which was a tremendous prize. And somehow, the attention came to others that I'm a gifted boy. And so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Adler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Adler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adler? I didn't spoke about Adler yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't speak yet. But you said it came to Adler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Attention of others not Adler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of others. And through Edelstein and through my uncle, who was from Brunn. There was a very famous painter of the ghetto, Professor Otto Ungar. And he gave me private lessons. What does it mean, private lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only then you'll realized that you could paint? It was before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. Even before. Even before the deportation. As a matter of fact, in Ostrava, when things became bad and we knew the deportation is very near, Leo Haas, the famous painter from the ghettos, he was living near Mahrisch Ostrau. Then he came to Mahrisch Ostrau. And he gave me his first lessons in drawing. Later on, he gave me also-- he had for a child of 13, 14, to encourage me in Theresienstadt. And later, I got private lessons also from a Fleischmann. Fleischmann was the head of the-- we would call it, like, minister of hells. But he was a doctor, an artist, and a writer and a poet. And he showed me his office that was in a special room. After the work, I could come to him, show him my work. He criticized it and helped me. That was Fleischmann. And Fritta I met at Edelstein's. And these were most of them the artists of Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Painters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because this artist worked in the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a special unit who prepared drawings and illustrations for the camp. And also, for the [NON-ENGLISH]. How you would call it for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, they had to give a [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Report.

speaker: interviewee
text: A kind of a report for the Germans. And this has to be done in a special way that is suitable for the German. And there was also Spero, I think, a Dutch painter with some very sweet drawings for these Germans, and who also designed stamps--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these reports were for the Germans? And they were in big--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And in this [SPEAKING GERMAN], or how you called it, this Spero-- they had all the materials which they needed for the work. And I got secretly some of the material for my own use. By the way, you know that these painters have done a lot of painting illegal. And many of their paintings survived. But you know that they were once caught. And the German got this illegal paintings, which showed all the horror and terrible condition of Theresienstadt. And they were sent to the small forest, which was a concentration camp near to Theresienstadt for politic prisoners. A very bad place. It had nothing to do with the ghetto, Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these painters came there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Political, yes. Political prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And were tortured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of them were killed. And only Ungar survived. They broke his fingers and so on. But he survived and died a short few weeks after liberation. And Leo Haas survived and lived till the age of 80 in Prague. And later on, in East Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Fleischmann?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fleischmann was killed later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had good teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had the best available at that time. Of course, also in that children room, and the home, there were some artists. I remember Ilka Schubert, who died. But he was a painter and a youth leader. And he also helped me because I was in the same home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, there are these famous paintings of children from Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, these were my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I actually would have also some of the drawings there. But most of the drawings I took with me to Auschwitz. And when I arrived--

speaker: interviewer
text: They perished?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some of the drawing are somewhere in the Jewish Museum, because the director of the Jewish Museum now told me he saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish Museum-- which?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague. But they can't find them for one reason or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there is this book which says, There Are No Butterflies Here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I organized the first exhibition at Yad Vashem of these drawings. These all were my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And these originals are where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Prague museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: They survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we shall stop, and we shall continue soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now we have our--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
